skill.name: antfu
skill.description: Apply Anthony Fu opinionated web development preferences for TypeScript, Vue, pnpm, ESLint, testing, and project setup.
skill.summary: Use Anthony Fu style conventions for modern TypeScript and Vue projects.
skill.author: antfu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily over-counted Markdown examples as Ruby backticks, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and credential access. Most findings are false positives from documentation and code snippets, but the skill does recommend package-manager commands and third-party reusable GitHub Actions workflows, including release permissions that require user review.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Reusable Release Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends sxzz/workflows for GitHub Actions and a release workflow with contents: write and id-token: write. This is not malicious by itself, but it creates supply-chain and publishing risk if users add it without reviewing the referenced workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly recommends a third-party reusable workflow and grants write and OIDC permissions. The context is legitimate CI setup, so this is a review-required supply-chain risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Project Commands and Hook Scripts Require Approval
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends package-manager commands, npx commands, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and release scripts. These are normal development instructions, but generated project automation can execute dependencies and should be approved before running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and would execute local or installed tooling if adopted. I found no command injection or hidden exfiltration, so the risk is operational and consent-based.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Span False Positives
low_findings.description: The many external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts are caused by Markdown code spans, file names, rule names, and framework examples. I found no executable skill script, dynamic code execution, or malicious instruction in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is documentation syntax and web development examples, not active code in the skill. The surrounding context explains formatting, Vue conventions, and configuration choices.

low_findings.title: Environment File Reference Is Protective
low_findings.description: The sensitive .env finding is a .gitignore recommendation that excludes environment files from version control. This reduces accidental secret disclosure and is not evidence of environment variable access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The .env entry appears only inside a .gitignore template. There is no code reading, printing, or transmitting environment variables.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Example Is Scoped Configuration Generation
low_findings.description: The Node.js filesystem operations read and update tsconfig.alias.json to keep TypeScript path aliases synchronized. This is local project configuration code with no network sink or credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example uses fixed local paths and writes a derived config file. It should still be reviewed before adoption, but I found no evidence of destructive writes or data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reusable Workflow With Publishing Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated GitHub Actions can delegate release behavior to a third-party repository while granting contents: write and id-token: write. Users should pin and review the workflow before enabling publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission grant is explicit and relevant to package publishing. The finding is not critical because the workflow is documented openly and not presented as stealth behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Scripts Execute Development Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: Recommended scripts and hooks can run package-manager, linting, build, and release tools. This is expected for a coding skill, but execution should remain user-approved.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts are directly recommended in project configuration examples. I did not find evidence that the skill itself runs them automatically.

content.value_statement: Modern JavaScript projects need consistent tooling choices across linting, testing, builds, and release setup. This skill applies Anthony Fu opinionated conventions for TypeScript, Vue, pnpm, ESLint, Vitest, and related project structure.
actual_capabilities: Recommends pnpm, ESM, strict TypeScript, and @antfu/ni command conventions.
Generates @antfu/eslint-config setup examples for TypeScript, Vue, React, Svelte, Astro, Solid, and UnoCSS projects.
Suggests Vitest, simple-git-hooks, lint-staged, and VitePress project configuration patterns.
Provides guidance for Vue app structure, library publishing with tsdown, and pnpm monorepos.
Creates recommended .gitignore and VS Code extension configuration for JavaScript and TypeScript projects.
Documents GitHub Actions workflow templates for autofix, unit tests, and tag-based releases.
limitations: It reflects one opinionated development style and may not match every team standard.
It provides configuration guidance but does not verify dependency versions or security advisories.
Release workflow recommendations require review before use in production repositories.
Framework coverage focuses on modern JavaScript, TypeScript, Vue, and related tooling.

use_cases.title: Start a TypeScript Web App
use_cases.description: Set up Vue, Vite or Nuxt, UnoCSS, strict TypeScript, and linting using the documented preferences.

use_cases.title: Standardize a JavaScript Monorepo
use_cases.description: Apply pnpm workspaces, shared scripts, centralized aliases, and consistent linting across multiple packages.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Library for Publishing
use_cases.description: Use tsdown, ESM-only package settings, Vitest, release scripts, and GitHub Actions templates.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Project Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new TypeScript project
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the antfu preferences to this new TypeScript project. Add the recommended package manager, strict TypeScript settings, ESLint config, and basic scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Configure a Vue Application
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Vue app and update it toward the antfu style. Focus on script setup, TypeScript props and emits, UnoCSS, VueUse, and linting.

prompt_templates.title: Modernize a Library Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this TypeScript library to the antfu publishing style. Use pure ESM, tsdown, generated declarations, Vitest, and clear release scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Monorepo Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare this monorepo against the antfu conventions. Propose changes for pnpm workspaces, catalogs, recursive scripts, aliases, testing, and CI. Flag any risky automation before adding it.

output_examples.input: Set up linting for a new TypeScript app.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the @antfu/eslint-config setup, adds a flat ESLint config, suggests a lint script, and notes when extra framework plugins are needed.

output_examples.input: Prepare a package for ESM library publishing.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends tsdown, ESM package fields, declaration output, Vitest, prepack builds, and a release script with review notes.

output_examples.input: Add CI to a repository using this style.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines autofix, unit test, and release workflows, then calls out third-party workflow and publishing permission review before enabling release automation.

best_practices: Review generated scripts, hooks, and CI workflows before running them or committing them.
Use the skill when you want one consistent style across TypeScript, Vue, linting, tests, and release setup.
Pin and inspect third-party GitHub Actions workflows before granting write or OIDC permissions.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every recommendation blindly to projects with established team standards.
Do not enable release workflows without reviewing publishing credentials and repository permissions.
Do not run package-manager or npx commands from generated output without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps apply Anthony Fu opinionated preferences for modern TypeScript, Vue, pnpm, ESLint, testing, documentation, and publishing workflows.

faq.question: Does it replace project-specific architecture decisions?
faq.answer: No. It provides tooling and convention guidance, but project architecture still needs product and team judgment.

faq.question: Is this only for Vue projects?
faq.answer: No. It includes Vue app guidance, but also covers TypeScript libraries, monorepos, ESLint, Vitest, VitePress, and GitHub Actions.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-focused, but it recommends executable scripts and third-party release workflows with write permissions.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users review before applying changes?
faq.answer: Users should review package-manager commands, generated hooks, GitHub Actions permissions, and release automation before enabling them.
